{"dataset_type": "Instructables", "data_id": "Instructables_56397", "url_id": "SLA-Textile-3D-Print", "title": "SLA Textile 3D Print", "summary_info": [{"type": "image", "url": "https://content.instructables.com/FEF/B8ZN/IZT6HJ0M/FEFB8ZNIZT6HJ0M.gif", "image_id": "FEFB8ZNIZT6HJ0M.gif", "image_path": "Instructables/cover_images/FEFB8ZNIZT6HJ0M.gif", "id": "FEFB8ZNIZT6HJ0M"}], "step_info": [[{"type": "text", "content": "SLA Textile 3D PrintThis post is a continuation of my previous Instructable about 3D Printing pre-rolled sheets on the Formlabs Form 2 3D printer, with durable resin. "}, {"type": "image", "url": "https://content.instructables.com/FDK/5QQZ/IZT6HHZV/FDK5QQZIZT6HHZV.gif", "image_id": "FDK5QQZIZT6HHZV", "image_path": "Instructables/Workshop/FDK5QQZIZT6HHZV.gif", "id": "FDK5QQZIZT6HHZV"}, {"type": "text", "content": "Read about it here . "}, {"type": "image", "url": "https://content.instructables.com/FTV/GDB4/IZT6HHYG/FTVGDB4IZT6HHYG.gif", "image_id": "FTVGDB4IZT6HHYG", "image_path": "Instructables/Workshop/FTVGDB4IZT6HHYG.gif", "id": "FTVGDB4IZT6HHYG"}, {"type": "text", "content": "Instead of printing tiled surfaces, I decided to generate a regular triangular lattice, but the rest of the process is fundamentally the same. "}, {"type": "image", "url": "https://content.instructables.com/FZN/JZOL/IZT6HHYX/FZNJZOLIZT6HHYX.gif", "image_id": "FZNJZOLIZT6HHYX", "image_path": "Instructables/Workshop/FZNJZOLIZT6HHYX.gif", "id": "FZNJZOLIZT6HHYX"}], [{"type": "text", "content": "CADWhat you'll need: 1. Form 2 2.Rhino 3D 3. Grasshopper plugin 4. Intralattice plugin for grasshopper Follow the instructions from the previous instructable . Or begin directly with the attached grasshopper script (spiral_intralattice.gh), rhino file, and STL (Lattice_textile.STL). When you open spiral_intralattice.gh, you should find input surface 1 and 2. Model any surface, offset it, and apply both surfaces to the grasshopper file, it will then generate a latticed surface that matches the topology you made. In my case, I modeled a simple spiral so that I could print a long roll. Notes: The new geometry allows for thicker members (1 mm) but more pliability. Additionally, there is no post-curing involved, and instead I recommend just leaving the part to dry in the sun. There is also no need for a support structure, since the model consists of its own triangular lattice structure. Enjoy!"}, {"type": "image", "url": "https://content.instructables.com/FEQ/UV6U/IZT6HI4L/FEQUV6UIZT6HI4L.png", "image_id": "FEQUV6UIZT6HI4L", "image_path": "Instructables/Workshop/FEQUV6UIZT6HI4L.png", "id": "FEQUV6UIZT6HI4L"}]], "content": [{"type": "text", "content": "SLA Textile 3D PrintThis post is a continuation of my previous Instructable about 3D Printing pre-rolled sheets on the Formlabs Form 2 3D printer, with durable resin. "}, {"type": "image", "url": "https://content.instructables.com/FEQ/UV6U/IZT6HI4L/FEQUV6UIZT6HI4L.png", "image_id": "FEQUV6UIZT6HI4L", "image_path": "Instructables/Workshop/FEQUV6UIZT6HI4L.png", "id": "FEQUV6UIZT6HI4L"}, {"type": "text", "content": "CADWhat you'll need: 1. Form 2 2.Rhino 3D 3. Grasshopper plugin 4. Intralattice plugin for grasshopper Follow the instructions from the previous instructable . Or begin directly with the attached grasshopper script (spiral_intralattice.gh), rhino file, and STL (Lattice_textile.STL). When you open spiral_intralattice.gh, you should find input surface 1 and 2. Model any surface, offset it, and apply both surfaces to the grasshopper file, it will then generate a latticed surface that matches the topology you made. In my case, I modeled a simple spiral so that I could print a long roll. Notes: The new geometry allows for thicker members (1 mm) but more pliability. Additionally, there is no post-curing involved, and instead I recommend just leaving the part to dry in the sun. There is also no need for a support structure, since the model consists of its own triangular lattice structure. Enjoy!"}, {"type": "image", "url": "https://content.instructables.com/FDK/5QQZ/IZT6HHZV/FDK5QQZIZT6HHZV.gif", "image_id": "FDK5QQZIZT6HHZV", "image_path": "Instructables/Workshop/FDK5QQZIZT6HHZV.gif", "id": "FDK5QQZIZT6HHZV"}], "answer": [1, 0], "num_images": 2, "num_placeholders": 2}
